342	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
343	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
344	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
345	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:

348	  %  satisfied by a finite set of

350	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
351	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
352	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
353	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
354	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
355	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
356	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
357	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
358	  %         p ∈ Set.range
359	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
360	  % NOTE (iter-008 encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
361	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
362	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
363	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
364	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
365	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
366	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
367	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
368	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
369	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
370	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
371	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
372	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
373	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).

376	\begin{proof}
377	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
378	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
379	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
380	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
381	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
382	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
383	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
384	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
385	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
386	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
387	\end{proof}

422	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
423	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
424	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
425	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
426	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
427	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
428	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
429	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
430	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
431	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
432	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
433	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
434	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
435	  normalisation with denominators).}
436	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
437	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
438	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
439	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
440	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
441	  \[
442	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
443	  \]
444	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
445	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
446	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
447	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
448	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
449	\end{lemma}
450	
451	\begin{proof}
452	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
453	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
454	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
455	  % NOTE (iter-007 decomposition): the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
456	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
457	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
458	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
459	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
460	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
461	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
462	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
463	
464	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
465	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
466	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
467	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
468	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
469	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
470	
471	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
472	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
473	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
474	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
475	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
476	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
477	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
478	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
479	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
480	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
481	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
482	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
483	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
484	
485	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
486	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending
487	  \(X_j \mapsto b_j\); the algebraic independence of the \(b_j\) over \(A_g\) (inherited
488	  from their independence over \(K\)) makes \(\varphi\) injective, and the
489	  module-finiteness just established is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over the
490	  image of \(\varphi\).
491	\end{proof}

504	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
505	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$

511	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
512	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
513	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :

515	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),

522	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
523	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
524	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
525	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
526	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
527	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
528	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
529	  \[
530	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
531	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.

538	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of

541	
542	\begin{proof}
543	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
544	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
545	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
546	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
547	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
548	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
549	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
550	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
551	
552	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
553	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
554	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
555	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
556	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
557	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
558	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
559	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
560	
561	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
562	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
563	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
564	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
565	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
566	\end{proof}

578	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
579	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
580	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
581	  \mathlibok
582	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
583	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
584	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
585	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
586	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
587	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
588	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
589	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}

591	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
592	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
593	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
594	\end{lemma}

596	\begin{lemma}\leanok
597	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
598	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
600	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
601	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
602	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
603	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
604	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
605	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
606	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
607	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
608	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
609	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
610	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
611	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
612	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
613	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
614	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
615	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
616	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
617	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
618	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
619	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
620	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
621	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
622	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
623	\end{lemma}
624	
625	\begin{proof}
626	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
627	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
628	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
629	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
630	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
631	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
632	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
633	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
634	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
635	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
636	\end{proof}

638	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
639	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
640	
641	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
642	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
643	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
644	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
645	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
646	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
647	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
648	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
649	
650	\begin{definition}[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
651	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
652	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
653	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
654	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
655	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
656	  \[
657	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
658	  \]
659	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
660	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
661	\end{definition}
662	
663	\begin{lemma}[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
664	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
665	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
666	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
667	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
668	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
669	\end{lemma}
670	\begin{proof}
671	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
672	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
673	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\). Proved directly
674	  in Lean.
675	\end{proof}
676	
677	\begin{definition}[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
678	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
679	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
680	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
681	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
682	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
683	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
684	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
685	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
686	\end{definition}
687	
688	\begin{lemma}[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
689	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
690	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
691	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
692	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
693	\end{lemma}
694	\begin{proof}
695	  Proved directly in Lean.
696	\end{proof}
697	
698	\begin{lemma}[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
699	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
701	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
702	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
703	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
704	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
705	\end{lemma}
706	\begin{proof}
707	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
708	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
709	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
710	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
711	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
712	\end{proof}
713	
714	\begin{lemma}[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
715	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
716	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
717	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
718	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
719	  transformed monomial is the radix value
720	  \[
721	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
722	  \]
723	\end{lemma}
724	\begin{proof}
725	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
726	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
727	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
728	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
729	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
730	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
731	\end{proof}
732	
733	\begin{lemma}[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
734	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
735	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
736	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
737	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
738	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
739	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
740	\end{lemma}
741	\begin{proof}
742	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
743	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
744	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
745	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
746	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
747	\end{proof}
748	
749	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
750	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
751	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
752	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
753	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
754	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
755	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
756	\end{lemma}
757	\begin{proof}
758	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
759	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
760	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
761	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
762	\end{proof}
763	
764	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
765	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
766	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
767	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
768	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
769	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
770	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
771	\end{lemma}
772	\begin{proof}
773	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
774	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
775	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
776	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
777	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
778	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
779	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
780	\end{proof}

782	\begin{lemma}[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
783	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
784	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
785	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
786	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
787	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
788	\end{lemma}
789	\begin{proof}
790	  Proved directly in Lean by induction on \(q\).
791	\end{proof}

793	\begin{lemma}[Constant inclusion under the variable-splitting isomorphism]
794	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
795	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}

797	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) (sending \(X_j \mapsto X_j\))
798	  with the isomorphism \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) sends \(s\)
799	  to the constant polynomial \(s \in (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\).
800	\end{lemma}

803	\end{proof}

805	\begin{lemma}\leanok
806	[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
807	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
808	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
809	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
810	  % NOTE (variable convention): the project's univariate elimination uses
811	  %   MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv, which singles out variable X_0 as the polynomial
812	  %   variable over the coefficient ring MvPolynomial (Fin n) A. The choice of which of
813	  %   the d = n+1 variables is "distinguished/eliminated" is immaterial (a renaming);
814	  %   the informal prose elsewhere says "last variable X_d" but the Lean uses X_0.
815	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
816	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
817	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
818	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
819	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
820	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
821	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
822	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
823	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g; map := MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A A_g).
824	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
825	  %       (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
826	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
827	  %         (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A),
828	  %         IsUnit
829	  %           (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) n
830	  %             (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff
831	  %   The automorphism e is the triangular Nagata substitution; g is the (non-zero)
832	  %   leading coefficient that becomes a unit after inversion.
833	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
834	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
835	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
836	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
837	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
838	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
839	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
840	\end{lemma}
841	
842	\begin{proof}
843	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
844	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
845	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
846	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
847	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
848	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
849	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
850	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
851	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
852	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
853	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
854	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
855	\end{proof}

863	  \(S[X]/(f)\) is a module-finite \(S\)-module (indeed free of rank \(\deg f\)).

969	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
970	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
971	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
972	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
973	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
974	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
975	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
976	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).

980	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
981	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
982	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
983	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
984	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
985	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
986	
987	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
988	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
989	
990	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
991	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
992	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
993	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
994	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
995	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
996	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
997	
998	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
999	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1000	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1001	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1002	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1003	  \(A_g\).
1004	
1005	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1006	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1007	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1008	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1009	\end{proof}

1044	\begin{proof}

1046	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,

1049	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1050	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE (iter-007): the induction must generalise the base domain A
1051	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1052	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1053	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1054	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1055	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1056	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1057	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1058	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1059	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1060	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1061	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1062	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1063	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1064	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1065	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1066	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1067	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1068	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1069	
1070	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1071	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1072	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1073	
1074	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1075	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1076	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1077	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1078	  \begin{enumerate}
1079	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1080	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1081	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1082	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1083	      \(P_d\)-modules
1084	      \[
1085	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1086	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1087	      \]
1088	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1089	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1090	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1091	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1092	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1093	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1094	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1095	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1096	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1097	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1098	      to \(A_g\) (see the structure note above).
1099	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Writing \(h = a / g^k\) with
1100	      \(a \in A\), the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is isomorphic to \(A_{g a}\) and
1101	      \((T_g)_h\) to \(T_{g a}\); thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over \((A_g)_h\) transports
1102	      to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the localisation-tower free-transport
1103	      fact \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}. Set \(f := g a \ne 0\); then
1104	      \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1105	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1106	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1107	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1108	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1109	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1110	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1111	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1112	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1113	      completes the induction.
1114	  \end{enumerate}
1115	\end{proof}

1190	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1191	
1192	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1193	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1194	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1195	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1196	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1197	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1198	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1199	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1200	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1201	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1202	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1203	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1204	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1205	
1206	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1207	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1208	\end{theorem}
1209	
1210	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1211	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1212	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1213	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1214	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1215	% by restricting to a non-empty
1216	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1217	\begin{proof}
1218	  
1219	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1220	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1221	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1222	
1223	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1224	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1225	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1226	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1227	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1228	
1229	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1230	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1231	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-type hypothesis makes \(M_j\)
1232	  a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is
1233	  the data fed to the algebraic form.
1234	
1235	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1236	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1237	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1238	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1239	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1240	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1241	
1242	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1243	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the fibrewise flatness conclusion then follows from
1244	  these local freeness statements by the criterion that a module flat at every maximal
1245	  ideal is flat. This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1246	\end{proof}
1247	
1248	\section{Mathlib status}
1249	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1250	
1251	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1252	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1253	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1254	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1255	generic-flatness argument consumes
1256	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1257	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1258	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1259	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1260	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1261	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1262	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1263	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1264	are introduced in
1265	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
1266

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With the chart ring
137	  %   `R^I := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ`, define
138	  %   `universalMatrix d r I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I` whose entry at
139	  %   `(p, q)` is: the identity entry `if (orderIso I) p = q then 1 else 0`
140	  %   when `q ∈ I` (using the order isomorphism `Fin d ≃o I` from `#I = d`),
141	  %   and the indeterminate `MvPolynomial.X (p, ⟨q, hq⟩)` when `q ∉ I`.
142	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
143	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
144	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
145	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
146	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
147	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
148	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
149	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
150	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
151	\end{definition}
152	
153	\begin{definition}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
157	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
159	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
160	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
161	  % affine patches" (read from
162	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
164	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
165	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
166	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
167	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). `minorDet d r I J : R^I` is the
168	  %   determinant of the `d × d` submatrix of `universalMatrix d r I` whose
169	  %   columns are indexed by `J`, reindexed to `Fin d` via the order
170	  %   isomorphism `Fin d ≃o J` coming from `#J = d`:
171	  %   `(universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J) |>.det`.
172	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
173	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
174	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
175	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
176	  \[
177	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
178	  \]
179	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
180	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
181	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
182	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
183	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	\leanok
189	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
190	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
192	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
193	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
194	  % affine patches" (read from
195	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
196	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
197	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
198	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
199	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
200	  %   `universalMinor d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
201	  %   is the `J`-minor of `universalMatrix d r I` base-changed along the
202	  %   structure map `R^I → R^I_J = Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`:
203	  %   `((universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J)).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
204	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
205	  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
206	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
207	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
208	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
209	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
210	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
211	\end{definition}
212	
213	\begin{lemma}
214	
215	\leanok
216	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
217	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_det_universalMinor}
219	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
220	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
221	  % affine patches" (read from
222	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
223	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
224	  % "the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
225	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
226	  %  on $U^I_J$."
227	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
228	  %   `isUnit_det_universalMinor d r I J : IsUnit (universalMinor d r I J).det`.
229	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
230	  The determinant of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
231	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}) is a unit of \(R^I_J\):
232	  \[
233	    \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \in (R^I_J)^{\times}.
234	  \]
235	\end{lemma}
236	\begin{proof}
237	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
238	  \leanok
239	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_minor}, \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of \(P^I_J\)
240	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) under the structure map
241	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J\) is the localisation away from
242	  \(P^I_J\), that image is a unit by
243	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}.
244	\end{proof}
245	
246	\begin{definition}
247	
248	\leanok
249	[The Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)]
250	  \label{def:gr_universalMinorInv}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv}
252	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor}
253	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
254	  % affine patches" (read from
255	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
256	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
257	  % "the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
258	  %  on $U^I_J$."
259	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
260	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
261	  %   `:= (universalMinor d r I J)⁻¹` (the Mathlib nonsingular inverse).
262	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
263	  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the
264	  nonsingular (Cramer) inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
265	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
266	\end{definition}
267	
268	\begin{lemma}
269	
270	\leanok
271	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
272	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
274	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
275	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). A conjunction (or pair of
276	  %   lemmas):
277	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J * universalMinor d r I J = 1` and
278	  %   `universalMinor d r I J * universalMinorInv d r I J = 1`.
279	  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
280	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
281	  genuine two-sided inverse:
282	  \[
283	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
284	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
285	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
286	  \]
287	\end{lemma}
288	\begin{proof}
289	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
290	  \leanok
291	  Immediate from \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit} together with
292	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
293	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
294	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
295	\end{proof}
296	
297	\begin{definition}
298	
299	\leanok
300	[The image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)]
301	  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
303	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
304	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
305	  % affine patches" (read from
306	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "The images of the variables
309	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
310	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
311	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
312	  %   `imageMatrix d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
313	  %   `:= universalMinorInv d r I J * (universalMatrix d r I).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
314	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
315	  Define the \(d \times r\) matrix over \(R^I_J\)
316	  \[
317	    M \;:=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I \;\in\; \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times r}(R^I_J),
318	  \]
319	  the product of the Cramer inverse (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) with the
320	  universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) base-changed to
321	  \(R^I_J\). Its entries are the prospective images of the indeterminates
322	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) under \(\theta_{I,J}\). Its \(J\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J =
323	  1_{d\times d}\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so \(M\) is a
324	  point of the chart \(U^J\); and its \(I\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I =
325	  (X^I_J)^{-1}\).
326	\end{definition}

328	\begin{definition}
329	
330	\leanok
331	[The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\)]
332	  \label{def:gr_transition_pre}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap}
334	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix}
335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
336	  % affine patches" (read from
337	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
338	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
339	  % "a ring homomorphism
340	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
341	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
342	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
343	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
344	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Via the universal property of the
345	  %   polynomial chart ring `R^J := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ J}) ℤ`,
346	  %   `transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →ₐ[ℤ] Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
347	  %   `:= MvPolynomial.aeval (fun e => imageMatrix d r I J e.1 e.2.1)`, i.e. the
348	  %   free indeterminate `x^J_{p,q}` is sent to the `(p,q)`-entry of `imageMatrix`.
349	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
350	  Let \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) be the
351	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out of the chart ring of \(J\) determined,
352	  via the universal property of the polynomial ring, by sending each free
353	  indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the image matrix \(M =
354	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}); equivalently
355	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) as a matrix identity (the
356	  identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\) matching the identity \(J\)-minor of \(M\)).
357	\end{definition}

359	\begin{lemma}
360	
361	\leanok
362	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
363	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
365	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
366	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
367	  % affine patches" (read from
368	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
370	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
371	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
372	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
373	  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
374	  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
376	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
377	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
378	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
379	  \[
380	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
381	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
382	  \]
383	  which is a unit.
384	\end{lemma}
385	\begin{proof}
386	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
387	  \leanok
388	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
389	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
390	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
391	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
392	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
393	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
394	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
395	\end{proof}
396	
397	\begin{definition}
398	
399	\leanok
400	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
401	  \label{def:gr_transition}
402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
403	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
404	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
405	  % affine patches" (read from
406	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
407	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
408	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
409	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
410	  %  defined as follows. ...
411	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
412	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
413	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The away-localisation lift of the
414	  %   pre-hom along `P^J_I`:
415	  %   `transitionMap d r I J :`
416	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
417	  %   `:= IsLocalization.Away.lift (minorDet d r J I)`
418	  %   `(isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J)` applied to
419	  %   `(transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →+* R^I_J)`.
420	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
421	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
422	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
423	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
424	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
425	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
426	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
427	  \[
428	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
429	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
430	  \]
431	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
432	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
433	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
434	\end{definition}
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	\leanok
439	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
440	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
442	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
443	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
444	  % affine patches" (read from
445	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
447	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
448	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
449	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
450	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
451	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
452	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
453	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
454	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
455	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
456	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
457	\end{lemma}
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
460	  \leanok
461	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
462	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
463	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
464	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
465	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
466	\end{proof}

468	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
469	
470	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
471	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
472	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	[Column submatrix of a product]
476	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
478	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
479	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
480	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
481	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
482	  the right factor:
483	  \[
484	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
485	  \]
486	\end{lemma}
487	\begin{proof}
488	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
489	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
490	\end{proof}
491	
492	\begin{lemma}
493	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
494	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
495	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
496	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
497	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
498	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
499	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
500	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
501	  \[
502	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
503	  \]
504	\end{lemma}
505	\begin{proof}
506	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
507	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
508	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
509	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
510	\end{proof}
511	
512	\begin{lemma}
513	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
514	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
516	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
517	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
518	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
519	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
520	\end{lemma}
521	\begin{proof}
522	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
523	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
524	\end{proof}
525	
526	\begin{lemma}
527	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
528	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
529	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
530	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
531	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
532	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
533	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
534	  \[
535	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
536	  \]
537	\end{lemma}
538	\begin{proof}
539	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
540	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
541	\end{proof}
542	
543	\begin{lemma}
544	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
545	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
547	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
548	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
549	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
550	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
551	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
552	  defining the chart.)
553	\end{lemma}
554	\begin{proof}
555	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
556	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
557	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
558	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
559	\end{proof}
560	
561	\begin{lemma}
562	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
563	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
565	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
566	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
567	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
568	  \[
569	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
570	  \]
571	\end{lemma}
572	\begin{proof}
573	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
574	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
575	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
576	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
577	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
578	\end{proof}
579	
580	\begin{lemma}
581	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
582	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
583	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
584	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
585	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
586	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the Cramer inverse:
587	  \[
588	    M_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}.
589	  \]
590	\end{lemma}
591	\begin{proof}
592	  By \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col} the \(I\)-column submatrix of \(M\) is
593	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\), and \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
594	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
595	\end{proof}
596	
597	\begin{lemma}
598	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
599	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
600	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
601	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
602	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
603	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
604	  the image matrix:
605	  \[
606	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
607	  \]
608	\end{lemma}
609	\begin{proof}
610	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
611	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
612	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
613	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the
614	  corresponding entry of \(M_J = 1_{d\times d}\)
615	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
616	\end{proof}
617	
618	\begin{lemma}
619	[Image of a cross minor under \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)]
620	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
621	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet}
622	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
623	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
624	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
625	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
626	  \[
627	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
628	  \]
629	\end{lemma}
630	\begin{proof}
631	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
632	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
633	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
634	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.
635	\end{proof}
636	
637	\begin{lemma}
638	[Naturality of the image matrix]
639	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
641	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
642	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
643	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
644	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
645	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
646	  \[
647	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
648	  \]
649	\end{lemma}
650	\begin{proof}
651	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
652	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
653	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
654	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
655	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
656	  Proved directly in Lean.
657	\end{proof}
658	
659	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
660	
661	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
662	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
663	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
664	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
665	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
666	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
667	\[
668	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
669	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
670	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
671	\]
672	
673	\begin{definition}
674	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
675	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
676	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
677	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}

827	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}

829	  \uses{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_universal_minor}

831	  equals \(\Theta_{I,J}\) applied entrywise to the image matrix of
832	  \(\theta_{J,K}\):
833	  \[
834	    \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_\ast\bigl((X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\bigr)
835	      \;=\;
836	      \bigl(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\bigr)_\ast
837	        \bigl((X^J_K)^{-1} X^J\bigr) .
838	  \]
839	  Both sides reduce to \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\), where \(Y\) is the universal matrix
840	  \(X^I\) base-changed to \(S_I\) and \(Y_K\) its \(K\)-minor.

844	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}, via \cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}).
845	  For the right side, \(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\) carries the chart
846	  ring \(R^J\) over \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}\circ\tilde\theta_{I,J}\), so the image
847	  matrix of \(\theta_{J,K}\) maps to \((M^J_K)^{-1} M^J\) with
848	  \(M^J = (Y_J)^{-1} Y\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap},
849	  \cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}); the minor \(M^J_K = (Y_J)^{-1} Y_K\)

882	  %   does NOT typecheck. `transitionMap d r J K` has codomain

884	  %   `transitionMap d r I J` has domain

891	  %     S_K := Localization.Away (minorDet d r K I hK hI * minorDet d r K J hK hJ)  -- R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)]

894	  %   With the canonical `algebraMap`s from each single away-localisation into the
895	  %   matching double one, the localised transition maps
896	  %     Θ_{J,K} : S_K →+* S_J,  Θ_{I,J} : S_J →+* S_I,  Θ_{I,K} : S_K →+* S_I
897	  %   are the `IsLocalization.Away.lift`s (equivalently the `IsLocalization.lift`s of
898	  %   `transitionMap …` along the extra inverted minor) of the respective
899	  %   `transitionPreMap` composed with the structure map into the double localisation.
900	  %   The conclusion is the propositional equality of ring homs `S_K →+* S_I`:
901	  %     `cocycleCondition d r I J K (hI : I.card = d) (hJ : J.card = d) (hK : K.card = d) :`
902	  %       `Θ_{I,K} = Θ_{I,J}.comp Θ_{J,K}`.
903	  %   (It is acceptable to pin the conclusion over the chart ring S_I on the

906	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
907	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
908	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
909	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
910	  \[
911	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
912	  \]
913	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
914	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
915	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
916	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
917	  glue the charts.

921	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
922	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
923	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
924	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
925	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
926	% L838-L848.)
927	\begin{proof}
928	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
929	  \leanok
930	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
931	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
932	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
933	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
934	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
935	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
936	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
937	
938	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
939	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
940	  \[
941	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
942	  \]
943	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
944	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
945	  \[
946	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
947	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
948	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
949	  \]
950	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
951	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
952	  \[
953	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
954	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
955	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
956	  \]
957	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
958	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
959	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
960	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
961	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
962	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
963	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
964	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
965	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
966	\end{proof}
