972	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
973	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
974	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
975	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
976	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
977	  % affine patches" (read from
978	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
979	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
980	  % "Therefore the schemes
981	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
982	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
983	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
984	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
985	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
986	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
987	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
988	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
989	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
990	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
991	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
992	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
993	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
994	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
995	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
996	  \[
997	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
998	  \]
999	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1000	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1001	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1002	
1003	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1004	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1005	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1006	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1007	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1008	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1009	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1010	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1011	  follows.
1012	\end{definition}
1013	
1014	\section{Separatedness}
1015	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1016	
1017	\begin{lemma}
1018	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1019	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
1020	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
1021	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1022	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
1023	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
1024	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1025	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
1026	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
1027	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
1028	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1029	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1030	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1031	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
1032	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1033	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
1034	\end{lemma}
1035	
1036	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
1037	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
1038	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1039	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1040	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
1041	\begin{proof}
1042	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1043	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
1044	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
1045	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
1046	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
1047	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
1048	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
1049	  homomorphism is surjective.
1050	
1051	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
1052	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
1053	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
1054	  \[
1055	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1056	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
1057	    \qquad
1058	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1059	  \]
1060	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
1061	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
1062	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
1063	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
1064	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
1065	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
1066	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
1067	  and in particular
1068	  \[
1069	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
1070	  \]
1071	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
1072	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
1073	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
1074	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
1075	  \[
1076	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
1077	  \]
1078	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
1079	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
1080	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
1081	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
1082	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
1083	\end{proof}
1084	
1085	\section{Properness}
1086	\label{sec:gr_proper}
1087	
1088	\begin{lemma}
1089	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1090	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
1091	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
1092	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
1093	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from

1097	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.

1104	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore

1107	  %

1110	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
1111	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
1112	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
1113	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
1114	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
1115	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
1116	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
1117	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
1118	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1119	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
1120	  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
1121	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1122	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1123	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1124	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.
1125	\end{lemma}
1126	
1127	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
1128	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
1129	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
1130	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
1131	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
1132	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
1133	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
1134	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
1135	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
1136	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1137	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
1138	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
1139	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
1140	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
1141	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
1142	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
1143	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1144	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
1145	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1146	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
1147	\begin{proof}
1148	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
1149	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
1150	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
1151	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
1152	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
1153	  \[
1154	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1155	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
1156	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
1157	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
1158	    \end{array}
1159	  \]
1160	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
1161	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
1162	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1163	
1164	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
1165	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
1166	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
1167	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
1168	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
1169	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
1170	  \(K\). Define
1171	  \[
1172	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
1173	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
1174	  \]
1175	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
1176	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
1177	  landing in \(U^J\).
1178	
1179	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
1180	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
1181	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
1182	  \[
1183	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
1184	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
1185	    \qquad
1186	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
1187	  \]
1188	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
1189	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
1190	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
1191	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
1192	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
1193	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
1194	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
1195	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
1196	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
1197	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
1198	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
1199	
1200	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
1201	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
1202	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1203	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
1204	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
1205	  proper.
1206	\end{proof}

1208	\section{Out of scope}
1209	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
1210	
1211	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
1212	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
1213	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
1214	are not treated here:
1215	\begin{itemize}
1216	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
1217	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
1218	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
1219	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1220	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
1221	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
1222	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
1223	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
1224	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
1225	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
1226	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
1227	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
1228	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
1229	    that base change.
1230	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}

81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
347	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
349	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
350	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
351	    lem:ratHilb_bump}
352	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
353	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
354	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
355	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
356	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
357	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
358	  %  function
359	  %  $$
360	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
361	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
362	  %  $$
363	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
364	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
365	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
366	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
367	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
368	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
369	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
370	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
371	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
372	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
373	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
374	  such that for all \(n > N\)
375	  \[
376	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
377	  \]
378	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
379	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
380	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
381	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
382	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
383	
384	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
385	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
386	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
387	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
388	  the production of the rational series from the module.
389	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality infrastructure (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
390	  %   and axiom-clean. The present theorem is not yet a Lean declaration: it is blocked on a
391	  %   genuine Mathlib gap. Mathlib at the pinned commit carries no packaged graded-module quotient
392	  %   \(M/xM\), graded kernel of a degree-shifting endomorphism, or regrading over \(R/(x)\) with
393	  %   finite-module transfer. Closing the inductive reduction requires building that
394	  %   graded-quotient/kernel/regrading API, a substantial sub-build.
395	\end{lemma}
396	
397	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
398	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
399	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
400	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
401	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
402	%  following short exact sequence
403	%  $$
404	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
405	%  $$
406	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
407	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
408	\begin{proof}
409	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
410	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
411	    lem:ratHilb_bump}
412	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
413	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
414	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
415	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
416	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
417	
418	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
419	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
420	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
421	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
422	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
423	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). Then \(R\) is a graded
424	  quotient of a polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) with the \(x_i\) of
425	  degree \(1\); we induct on the minimal number \(r\) of degree-one generators.
426	
427	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} Here \(R = \kappa\) and \(M\) is a finitely
428	  generated \(\kappa\)-module, hence finite-dimensional, so \(M_n = 0\) and
429	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\). The Hilbert series
430	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is therefore a polynomial; taking \(d = 0\)
431	  and \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) the polynomial with these coefficients gives
432	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{0}\bigr)\) for all \(n\).
433	
434	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(x = x_r \in R_1\) be one of a minimal set of
435	  degree-one generators, so that the induction hypothesis applies to finitely
436	  generated graded modules over \(R/(x)\), itself a graded quotient of
437	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_{r-1}]\). Multiplication by \(x\) raises degree by one
438	  and assembles, for every \(n\), into a short exact sequence of
439	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector spaces
440	  \[
441	    0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{\;x\;} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0,
442	  \]
443	  where \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\) and \(C = M / xM\) are finitely
444	  generated graded \(R\)-modules annihilated by \(x\), hence finitely generated
445	  graded modules over \(R/(x)\). By additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact
446	  sequence (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}, applied to the kernel and
447	  cokernel of the map \(x\)),
448	  \[
449	    \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
450	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n
451	  \]
452	  for every \(n\). Multiplying the Hilbert series of \(M\) by \((1 - X)\) and
453	  summing this identity against \(X^{n}\) shows that
454	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) equals the Hilbert series of
455	  \(C\) minus that of \(K\), up to a polynomial in low degrees. By the induction
456	  hypothesis applied to the \(R/(x)\)-modules \(K\) and \(C\) (which live over
457	  \(r - 1\) variables), each of those Hilbert series is, up to a polynomial, of
458	  the form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\) with \(d - 1 \le r - 1\). Hence
459	  \[
460	    (1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
461	      \;=\; q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
462	  \]
463	  and multiplying by the inverse power series
464	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1) = (1 - X)^{-1}\)
465	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) gives
466	  \[
467	    \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
468	      \;=\; p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
469	  \]
470	  with \(d \le r\). Reading off the coefficient of \(X^n\) for \(n \gg 0\) yields
471	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) with
472	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\),
473	  which is the claim.
474	\end{proof}
475	
476	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
477	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
478	
479	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
480	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
481	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
482	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
483	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
484	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
485	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
486	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
487	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
488	
489	\begin{lemma}[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
490	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
491	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
492	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
493	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
494	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
495	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
496	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
497	  \(F\):
498	  \[
499	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
500	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
501	  \]
502	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
503	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
504	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
505	  the Cauchy product.
506	\end{lemma}
507	
508	\begin{lemma}[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
509	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
510	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
511	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
512	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
513	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
514	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
515	  \[
516	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
517	  \]
518	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
519	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
520	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
521	  \[
522	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
523	  \]
524	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
525	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
526	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
527	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
528	\end{lemma}
529	
530	\begin{proof}
531	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
532	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
533	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
534	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
535	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
536	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
537	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
538	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
539	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
540	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
541	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
542	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
543	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
544	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
545	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
546	\end{proof}
547	
548	\begin{definition}[Rational Hilbert function]
549	  \label{def:ratHilb}
550	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
551	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
552	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
553	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
554	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
555	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
556	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
557	  \[
558	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
559	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
560	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
561	  \]
562	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
563	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
564	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
565	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
566	\end{definition}
567	
568	\begin{lemma}[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
569	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
570	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
571	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
572	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
573	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
574	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
575	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
576	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
577	\end{lemma}
578	
579	\begin{lemma}[Raising the pole order]
580	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
582	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
583	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
584	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
585	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
586	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
587	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
588	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
589	\end{lemma}
590	
591	\begin{lemma}[Closure under difference]
592	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
593	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
594	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
595	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
596	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
597	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
598	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
599	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
600	\end{lemma}
601	
602	\begin{lemma}[Closure under right shift]
603	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
604	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
605	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
606	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
607	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
608	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
609	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
610	  Lean.
611	\end{lemma}
612	
613	\begin{lemma}[Antidifference step for the predicate]
614	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
615	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
616	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
617	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
618	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
619	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
620	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
621	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
622	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
623	\end{lemma}
624	
625	\begin{proof}
626	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
627	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
628	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
629	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
630	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
631	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
632	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
633	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
634	\end{proof}
635	
636	\begin{lemma}[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
637	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
638	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
639	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
640	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
641	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
642	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
643	  \[
644	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
645	  \]
646	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
647	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
648	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
649	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
650	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
651	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
652	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
653	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
654	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
655	\end{lemma}
656	
657	\begin{proof}
658	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
659	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
660	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
661	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
662	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
663	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
664	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
665	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
666	\end{proof}
667	
668	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
669	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
670	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
671	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
672	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
673	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
674	  % Polynomials" (read from
675	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
676	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
677	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
678	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
679	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
680	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
681	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
682	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
683	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
684	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
685	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
686	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
687	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
688	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
689	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
690	  \[
691	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
692	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
693	  \]
694	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
695	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
696	\end{theorem}
697	
698	\begin{proof}
699	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
700	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
701	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
702	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
703	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
704	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
705	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
706	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
707	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
708	  function
709	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
710	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
711	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
712	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
713	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
714	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
715	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
716	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
717	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
718	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
719	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
720	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
721	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
722	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
723	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
724	\end{proof}
725	
726	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
727	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
728	
729	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
730	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
731	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
732	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
733	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
734	
735	\begin{definition}
736	
737	\leanok
738	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
739	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
740	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
741	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
742	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
743	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
744	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
745	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
746	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
747	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
748	  % Polynomials" (read from
749	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
750	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
751	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
752	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
753	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
754	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
755	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
756	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
757	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
758	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
759	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
760	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
761	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
762	  \(U \subseteq X\),
763	  \[
764	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
765	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
766	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
767	  \]
768	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
769	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
770	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
771	
772	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
773	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
774	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
775	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
776	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
777	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
778	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
779	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
780	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
781	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
782	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
783	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
784	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
785	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
786	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
787	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
788	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
789	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
790	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
791	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
792	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
793	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
794	
795	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
796	\end{definition}
797	
798	\begin{lemma}[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
799	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
801	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
802	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
803	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
804	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
805	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
806	  \(U\):
807	  \[
808	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
809	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
810	  \]
811	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
812	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
813	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
814	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
815	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
816	\end{lemma}
817	
818	\begin{lemma}\leanok
819	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
820	  localization]
821	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
822	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
823	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
824	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
825	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
826	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
827	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
828	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
829	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
830	  \[
831	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
832	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
833	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
834	  \]
835	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
836	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
837	
838	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
839	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
840	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
841	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
842	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
843	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
844	\end{lemma}
845	
846	\begin{proof}
847	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
848	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
849	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
850	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
851	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
852	
853	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
854	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
855	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
856	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
857	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
858	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
859	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
860	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
861	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
862	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
863	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
864	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
865	\end{proof}
866	
867	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
868	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
869	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
870	  \mathlibok
871	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
872	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
873	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
874	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
875	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
876	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
877	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
878	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
879	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
880	\end{lemma}
881	
882	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
883	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
884	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
885	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
886	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
887	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
888	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
889	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
890	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
891	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
892	  %  associated to $M$.
893	  %  [...]
894	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
895	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
896	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
897	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
898	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
899	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
900	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
901	  \begin{enumerate}
902	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
903	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
904	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
905	    \item the section-restriction map
906	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
907	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
908	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
909	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
910	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
911	  \end{enumerate}
912	\end{lemma}
913	
914	\begin{proof}
915	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
916	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
917	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
918	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
919	
920	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
921	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
922	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
923	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
924	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
925	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
926	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
927	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
928	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
929	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
930	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
931	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
932	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
933	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
934	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
935	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
936	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
937	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
938	\end{proof}
939	
940	\begin{definition}
941	
942	\leanok
943	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
944	  \label{def:schematic_support}
945	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
946	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator, lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le,
947	    def:schematic_support_immersion}
948	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
949	  % Polynomials" (read from
950	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
951	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
952	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
953	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
954	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
955	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
956	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
957	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
958	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
959	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
960	  \[
961	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
962	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
963	  \]
964	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises
965	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
966	  \(X\).
967	\end{definition}
968

970	  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}

2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}

7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`

76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}
159	
160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  Constructed directly in Lean as the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); this is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}

229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
230	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
231	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
232	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
233	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
234	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
235	  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$
236	  Equivalently, for any \(X\)-scheme \(g : T \to X\),
237	  there is a natural bijection
238	  \[
239	    \Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \;\;\cong\;\;
240	    \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_* \mathcal{O}_T)
241	  \]
242	  (using the adjunction between \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) to convert between
243	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
244	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).
245	\end{theorem}

367	\section{Lean encoding}
368	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
369	
370	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
371	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
372	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
373	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
374	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
375	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
376	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
377	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
378	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
379	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
380	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
381	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
382	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
383	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
384	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
385	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
386	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
387	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
388	infrastructure.
389

986	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
987	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
988	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
989	  \mathlibok
990	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
991	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
992	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
993	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
994	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
995	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
996	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
997	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
998	\end{lemma}

1109	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert

1111	  \[

1113	  \]
